aspects.name: Read values from graph
aspects.description: Student reads literal values, identifies extremes, or compares quantities directly off a scatterplot, line graph, or table — no model construction or prediction beyond what is plotted is required. The cognitive operation is coordinate reading and visual comparison.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A1 stops at what the plotted points/segments say; A4 differs because A4 requires using a model line/curve to extrapolate or compute a residual; A3 differs because A3 reasons about the parameters (slope/intercept) of the fitted line, not the raw data.

types.name: Identify extremum / greatest change from line or scatter plot
types.description: A line graph or scatterplot is shown; student must identify the data point/interval with the maximum, minimum, fewest, or greatest year-over-year change.
types.core_skill_demand: Scan plotted values, compare them pairwise (or compute consecutive differences) and pick the option that meets a stated criterion (max, min, greatest change).

characteristics: Plot has ≤10 data points / years and the extreme value is visually obvious (clear peak or trough)
Single criterion (e.g., highest value, fewest grads) — no calculation required
Distractors are non-adjacent points so misreading the axis is unlikely
easy.example_stem: The line graph shows the probability of snow each day. For which day is the probability highest?

characteristics: Asks about an INTERVAL (between two consecutive values) requiring two reads plus subtraction
Differences between adjacent intervals are close in magnitude — student must compute several differences before deciding
Axis units use decimals or large numbers so a careful read is required
medium.example_stem: Between which consecutive days is the increase in temperature greatest?

characteristics: Not represented at Hard in this bank — pure extremum reading is too procedural.

must_preserve: Single plotted dataset (line graph or scatter) with a categorical/time x-axis
Question asks 'which X has the [greatest|least|fewest] Y' or '[greatest|smallest] change between consecutive'
All four MCQ options are points/intervals that appear on the plot
must_vary: Context (animals, sales, temperatures, sports stats, etc.)
Direction of trend (rising vs falling vs irregular)
Axis range/scale (small integers vs large counts vs percentages)
common_bugs: Cloner labels the plot but forgets to make distractors plausible — easy item becomes a giveaway
Cloner inserts a 'tie' for the extremum, making the answer non-unique
Cloner shifts to predicting a future value, drifting from A1 into A4

types.name: Read or compare values from a contextual graph
types.description: A non-scatter graph (line graph of speed vs time, temperature-vs-time curve, etc.) is given; the student must identify when a described behavior occurs (constant, decreasing, between which interval is X) — descriptive interpretation of plotted segments.
types.core_skill_demand: Translate verbal descriptions ('decreased at constant rate', 'change between') into segments of the plotted curve and pick matching option.

characteristics: Graph shape is piecewise-linear with clearly-distinguished segments
Question asks for a single property (where speed is constant, where greatest change occurs)
All distractors are explicit intervals labeled on the x-axis
easy.example_stem: According to the graph, which of the following statements is NOT true about Theresa's run?

must_preserve: Contextualized x-y graph (not a generic scatterplot)
Question targets a qualitative property of the graph (constant, decreasing, between which interval)
No equation is asked for
common_bugs: Cloner adds numerical computation, drifting into A4 (prediction)
Cloner makes two answer choices simultaneously true

aspects.name: Choose best-fit line equation
aspects.description: A scatterplot is shown; the student must pick from four MCQ candidate equations the one whose graph is the most appropriate linear (or in a few cases nonlinear) model for the displayed data. The cognitive operation is estimating slope sign+magnitude and y-intercept from the plot and matching to algebraic form.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A2 requires evaluating an EQUATION as a candidate for the data set; A3 differs because A3 asks for the numeric value of slope/intercept of a line already drawn; A5 differs because A5 asks which FAMILY (linear vs exponential vs increasing/decreasing) of model is appropriate, not which specific equation.

types.name: Pick linear equation y = mx + b matching scatterplot
types.description: Scatterplot shows points; four MCQ choices give candidate linear equations differing in slope sign and/or y-intercept sign. Student selects the equation whose slope direction and intercept match the visible trend.
types.core_skill_demand: Determine sign of slope from trend direction (up/down) and sign/magnitude of y-intercept from where line would cross x=0; eliminate equations with wrong sign on slope or intercept, then choose remaining option closest in magnitude.

characteristics: Slope is an obvious integer or simple decimal (e.g., 0.9, 1, -1)
All four MCQ options share the same magnitudes but differ in signs of slope/intercept — pure sign elimination suffices
Trend is sharply monotonic with little scatter
easy.example_stem: Which equation is the most appropriate linear model for the data shown in the scatterplot? A) y=-0.9x-2.2 B) y=-0.9x+2.2 C) y=-0.9x D) y=0.9x+2.2

characteristics: Options also vary the slope-intercept ROLES (which constant is slope vs intercept)
Slope is a non-integer decimal so estimating magnitude (not just sign) matters
Multiple options have correct slope sign — must pick correct intercept too
medium.example_stem: Which equation best models the scatterplot? y=0.9+9.4x vs y=9.4+0.9x vs y=9.4-0.9x ...

must_preserve: A scatterplot of 5-12 points with a clear linear trend
Exactly four MCQ candidate equations in y=mx+b (or y=b+mx) form
At least two distractors are sign-flipped versions of the correct equation
common_bugs: Cloner makes the correct option the only one with both correct signs — eliminating others is trivial; lower the discrimination by giving at least one distractor both signs correct but wrong magnitude
Cloner uses such a steep slope that the line would not fit the y-axis range shown

types.name: Pick best-fit equation in real context (named axes)
types.description: Like T3, but the scatterplot uses real-context axes (year, price, time, etc.) and the candidate equations use context variables. Slope and intercept must be estimated from the contextualized scale.
types.core_skill_demand: Same as T3 but with renamed variables and large-scale axes; must convert visual slope (∆y/∆x in actual units) to the coefficient in the equation and verify intercept matches starting context value.

characteristics: Variables are x and y but axes use small integer ranges; equations expressed simply
easy.example_stem: Which equation could be a line of best fit for the data?

characteristics: Context variables (t years since 1968, n visitors; height x, jump y); coefficients are non-integer
Distractor equations are close in slope magnitude — must compute ∆y/∆x precisely
Slope value must be matched to an axis with large range (1000s) so coefficient is small
medium.example_stem: t = years since 1968, n = visitors. Which equation could be a line of best fit?

characteristics: Both slope magnitude AND intercept location are tested; one distractor has correct slope but extrapolated intercept value (line's y-value at the right end of plot, not at x=0)
Axis does not start at the origin so intercept inference requires extrapolation
Plausible options differ only in which numerical value is intercept vs slope coefficient
hard.example_stem: Scatterplot passes through (0, 11.9) and (20, 42.6). Which equation could be the best fit? A) y=46.8+5.9x B) y=46.8+1.5x C) y=12+5.9x D) y=12+1.5x

must_preserve: Real-context scatterplot with renamed variables in equations
Four equations differing in intercept value AND slope coefficient
Hard variants must include a distractor whose intercept equals the line's value at the RIGHT end of the plot, not at x=0
must_vary: Whether trend is increasing or decreasing
common_bugs: Cloner uses an x-axis starting at 0 — collapses Hard back to Medium because intercept is directly visible
Cloner mismatches the equation's slope sign with the figure's trend direction

aspects.name: Quantify slope or intercept of fitted line
aspects.description: A scatterplot with an explicitly-drawn line of best fit is shown; the student must compute or estimate a single numerical parameter — the slope, the y-intercept, or the value of m or b — using two clearly indicated coordinates the line passes through.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A3 produces ONE numerical value about the line itself; A2 differs because A2 picks a whole equation; A4 differs because A4 plugs the line into a prediction or residual question. The boundary test: if the answer is a single slope or intercept number and the equation is not yet assembled, it is A3.

types.name: Compute slope of given best-fit line
types.description: Two coordinates the line of best fit passes through are described (e.g., (0, 5.6) and (12, 11)); student computes slope = ∆y/∆x and selects the value (often distractor options include the reciprocal, the negation, or the y-intercept value).
types.core_skill_demand: Identify two points on the drawn line, apply slope formula (y2-y1)/(x2-x1), choose sign by trend direction, and match to MCQ.

characteristics: Slope is a simple non-integer decimal computable from two integer-coordinate intersections (e.g., from (0,0) to (5,2.2) → 0.44)
Distractors include the sign-flipped slope and the reciprocal, so sign + direction matters
Trend is clearly monotonic
easy.example_stem: What is the slope of the line of best fit shown?

characteristics: Coordinates are decimal or use large axis ranges, so the student must compute ∆y/∆x where one or both differences are non-integer
Two distractors have correct slope magnitude but wrong sign, two have correct sign with wrong magnitude
Slope value is between 0 and 1 (or between -1 and 0), making sign+magnitude both diagnostic
medium.example_stem: The line passes through (0, 9) and (10, 2). What is the slope of the line of best fit?

characteristics: Not represented at Hard for pure slope: hard variants of this aspect move to T6 (slope interpretation in context).

must_preserve: Scatterplot with one explicit drawn line of best fit
Two specific coordinates the line passes through must be either visible at grid intersections or stated in the figure description
Question asks for the SLOPE (not 'the equation', which is A2)
must_vary: Whether the two reference points are at the axes or interior
common_bugs: Cloner edits the figure but forgets to update one of the reference coordinates, making the 'correct' slope inconsistent
Cloner picks line-passes-through points that don't match the depicted scatter trend

types.name: Interpret slope in context (units, per-unit change)
types.description: Given a contextual line of best fit (often with the equation displayed), student must interpret the slope as the predicted change in y per unit change in x, sometimes scaled (e.g., per 5 units, per 10 years).
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize slope = predicted ∆y per unit ∆x; if scaled, multiply slope by the requested step (e.g., 57 × 5 for a 5°C increase); express in correct units.

characteristics: Slope must be estimated from the figure first (no equation given) OR slope is given but must be scaled by a non-unit interval (e.g., per 5°C, per 10 years)
Distractors include the slope un-scaled, the slope scaled by the wrong factor, and the intercept value
Real-world interpretation phrasing makes literal application ambiguous
hard.example_stem: Slope of best fit is ~57. How many additional visitors are predicted for each 5°C increase?

must_preserve: Contextual scatterplot with line of best fit
Question targets the meaning of the slope (per-unit change, scaled change, or interpretation)
At least one distractor reflects un-scaled slope value
must_vary: Whether the slope is given or must be estimated
common_bugs: Cloner forgets to include a distractor for the un-scaled slope, making elimination trivial
Cloner mismatches units in answer with the slope's actual units

aspects.name: Predict / use model & residuals
aspects.description: Student uses the line of best fit (or model curve) to predict a y-value for a given x, or to compute the residual (actual minus predicted) at a specific data point. Requires applying the model, not merely describing it.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A4 always produces a NUMERICAL prediction or difference; A1 differs because A1 only reads plotted values without a model; A3 differs because A3 returns a parameter of the line itself.

types.name: Predict y at given x from line of best fit
types.description: Best-fit line drawn or equation given; student must use it to estimate y at a specified x-value (often an interpolated or modestly extrapolated x).
types.core_skill_demand: Locate x on the horizontal axis, trace vertically to best-fit line (or substitute x into equation), read corresponding y; select the closest MCQ value.

characteristics: Best-fit line is explicitly drawn; the x of interest lies inside the visible plot range
MCQ answers are spread far enough that small reading errors don't change the choice
Context uses everyday axes (temperature, distance, density)
easy.example_stem: What temperature does the line of best fit predict for an elevation of 6,200 feet?

must_preserve: Scatterplot with a clearly drawn line of best fit, OR a context with an equation
Student is asked to predict y at a specific x (interpolation or near extrapolation)
MCQ values close enough that careless reading is penalized but careful reading wins
must_vary: Whether x is inside or just outside the plotted range
common_bugs: Cloner extrapolates far beyond the data shown, making the question reduce to algebraic substitution into a stated equation (drifts toward T3/T4 logic without the figure)
Cloner makes two MCQ values equally close to the true value

types.name: Compute residual between actual data point and best-fit line
types.description: A specific data point on the scatterplot is identified (often by its x-coordinate or by being the maximum/minimum); student must compute the difference between its actual y and the y predicted by the line of best fit at that x.
types.core_skill_demand: Read actual y of the identified point; read predicted y from the line of best fit at the same x; subtract and take absolute value; select closest MCQ.

characteristics: Specific point is identified by extreme property (e.g., 'data point with greatest x', 'largest charity by %')
Both readings (actual and predicted) require careful gridline interpolation
Distractors include the actual y, the predicted y, the sum, and the correct difference
medium.example_stem: What is the difference between the y-value of the indicated point and the y predicted by the line of best fit?

must_preserve: Scatterplot with line of best fit AND at least one data point that differs noticeably from the line
Question asks for the DIFFERENCE between actual and predicted y at a specific x
Targeted point can be unambiguously identified (e.g., 'greatest x', '5th data point', a labeled point)
must_vary: Whether the targeted point is above or below the line
common_bugs: Cloner identifies a point by phrase that matches more than one point on the new scatter
Cloner reverses which value (actual vs predicted) is subtracted, flipping sign and breaking the MCQ

aspects.name: Identify model type (linear vs exponential)
aspects.description: Student classifies a relationship as linear vs exponential and (within that) as increasing vs decreasing, based on a context description, a table of values, or a graph. The cognitive operation is recognizing additive vs multiplicative growth and the sign of change.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A5 is purely categorical (4 model families); A2 differs because A2 chooses a specific equation, not a family; A1/A4 differ because A1/A4 use a model that has already been chosen.

types.name: Classify model from verbal context
types.description: A short context describes a process (constant-rate increase, percentage growth, depletion); student selects from {increasing linear, decreasing linear, increasing exponential, decreasing exponential}.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize 'constant rate / per unit' = linear; '% of previous / multiplied by factor' = exponential; sign of slope/growth-factor determines increasing vs decreasing.

characteristics: Verbatim 'constant rate' or 'every minute it drops by X' phrasing
Direction of change is explicit ('descends', 'increases')
Only one of the four options matches all conditions
easy.example_stem: Airplane descends at a constant rate of 400 feet per minute. What models its altitude over time?

characteristics: Growth rule uses a PERCENTAGE phrase ('increases by 0.49% of previous value') — must recognize multiplicative
Or describes depletion at constant rate (decreasing linear) with multiple distractors
Distractors include both wrong family AND wrong direction
medium.example_stem: Each year the value of an investment increases by 0.49% of its previous year's value. Which model applies?

characteristics: Wording obscures whether growth is multiplicative or additive (e.g., 'f(x) = 201% of x' — a linear function with slope 2.01, NOT exponential)
Compare two accounts (compound vs simple) — eventually crossover behavior, requires reasoning about long-run behavior
Multiple plausible options after surface reading
hard.example_stem: For x > 0, f(x) = 201% of x. Which of these describes this function?

must_preserve: Pure verbal context (no graph or table required to answer)
Four MCQ options spanning the 2x2 grid {linear, exponential} × {increasing, decreasing}
Process description that unambiguously points to one cell of the grid
must_vary: Whether growth is described in absolute units vs percentage
common_bugs: Cloner uses '% of x' phrasing (constant proportionality) confused with '% growth per period' (exponential) — produces wrong key
Cloner forgets to flip increasing/decreasing when sign of change is negative

types.name: Classify model from graph or table
types.description: A graph (curve or line) or a 4-row table of (x,y) values is shown; student picks from {increasing linear, decreasing linear, increasing exponential, decreasing exponential} or judges nonlinearity.
types.core_skill_demand: From a curve: identify whether it is straight (linear) or shows curvature/asymptote (exponential); from a table: check constant differences (linear) vs constant ratios (exponential).

characteristics: Graph is clearly a straight line OR clearly a curve with an asymptote
Table has only 3-4 rows with simple integer differences/ratios
Sign of change is obvious
easy.example_stem: The table shows selected values from f. Which of the following best describes f?

characteristics: Multiple tables shown; student must check which is nonlinear by computing differences
Compares two functions f and g across x ranges (when linear < exp, when linear > exp)
Distractors include reasonable misreadings (mistaking a slight curve for linear, or vice-versa)
medium.example_stem: In which of the following tables is the relationship between x and y nonlinear?

must_preserve: Visual representation (graph or table) showing the function
Option set covering the 2x2 model grid OR explicitly testing linearity
Decisive feature (constant difference, constant ratio, or visible curvature)
must_vary: Whether representation is a graph or a table
Whether the linear family or exponential family is correct
common_bugs: Cloner makes a table whose differences and ratios are both nearly constant — ambiguous answer
Cloner draws curve that is too subtle to distinguish from a straight line

types.name: Choose model family for given scatterplot
types.description: Scatterplot is shown with strongly nonlinear (often exponential) shape; student selects from candidate models the most appropriate fit, where options vary by family (line vs curve) or by direction/coverage.
types.core_skill_demand: Recognize that the data are not best modeled by a line; identify the curve type (exponential growth or decay) and pick the option whose described curve covers the data most appropriately.

characteristics: Data are visibly exponential (sharp curvature)
Distractors include linear-of-best-fit (wrong family) and curves with wrong direction
Correct answer is a curve that touches/passes near most points
easy.example_stem: Of the following, which is the best model for the data in the scatterplot?

must_preserve: Scatterplot with clearly nonlinear (typically exponential) trend
Four MCQ options differing in family (line vs curve) and orientation
Exactly one option is a well-fitting curve
common_bugs: Cloner uses near-linear data, allowing two answers (line or curve) to fit equally
Cloner forgets that correct option must touch/be near most data points

aspects.name: Quantify nonlinear (exponential) model parameter
aspects.description: Scatterplot has an explicit exponential curve of best fit. Student must identify a parameter (initial value, growth factor, or constant in y = a·b^x) by reading two points the curve passes through.
aspects.boundary_with_other_aspects: A6 is the exponential analog of A3; differs from A5 because A5 only picks the family, while A6 finds a specific parameter; differs from A2 because A2 picks an equation form but rarely solves for a parameter.

types.name: Identify exponential parameter from curve of best fit
types.description: Curve y = a·b^x is fitted to the scatterplot; student must select the numerical value of a parameter (initial y-value a, or growth factor b) by reading the curve at x=0 and one other x.
types.core_skill_demand: Read y at x=0 (gives a); read y at another x and solve b = (y/a)^(1/x); pick closest option (or identify a directly if intercept is asked).

characteristics: Curve uses non-integer initial value and growth/decay factor
Distractors include initial value (close to actual a), reciprocal of growth factor, and far-extrapolated values
Student must select among similar magnitudes (e.g., 0.83 vs 1.83 — both plausible as decay base)
hard.example_stem: The curve of best fit passes through (0, 126) and (10, 20). Which of the following could be the growth factor?

must_preserve: Scatterplot with an explicit exponential curve of best fit
Curve must pass through at least two clearly readable coordinates (one at x=0 ideally)
Question asks for a numerical parameter (initial value, growth/decay factor) NOT a whole equation
common_bugs: Cloner asks for a parameter that cannot be uniquely determined from the two stated points
Cloner provides distractor values that are not algebraically close (no plausible misstep produces them)

skill_wide_notes: All originals fall into 6 aspects: (A1) read raw values, (A2) pick best-fit equation, (A3) quantify slope/intercept of fitted line, (A4) predict / residual, (A5) classify model family, (A6) quantify exponential parameter. Hard items concentrate in A2-T4 (context equations), A3-T6 (slope interpretation), A5-T9 (subtle linear-vs-exponential wording), and A6-T12 (exponential curves). All scatterplot-based items have a corresponding 'aria-label' SVG description; cloners should preserve the descriptive long-form (the verifier relies on it to extract trend direction and coordinates). One Easy SPR (b58dbf88) asks for a numerical predicted weight from a nectarine-tree growth scatterplot and fits T7.
